coredata.dc:title: Electronic and magnetic properties of graphene quantum dots doped with alkali metals
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Osman, Waleed
Abdelsalam, H.
Ali, M.
Teleb, N.H.
Yahia, I.S.
Ibrahim, M.A.
Zhang, Q.
coredata.dc:description: The electronic and magnetic properties of armchair-hexagonal (AHEX) and zigzag-triangular (ZTRI) graphene quantum dots doped with alkali metals are investigated using density functional theory. The binding energy confirms the stability of the undoped systems. Although doping decreases stability of single layer structures, in bilayer ones the binding energy between the layers increases. The former is due to the broken bonds and the deformation at the surface, while the later is due to the chemical bonds formation between the layers. We found that the lowest ground state energy for AHEX/AHEX-doped is the singlet/quadruplet spin state. Therefore, AHEX dots experience transformation from diamagnetic to ferromagnetic state after doping. In addition, the optimized spin state for ZTRI/ZTRI-doped is the quintet/sextet spin-polarized state. In few cases, the doped AHEX/ZTRI dots have doublet/quadruplet spin state due to the strong interaction between the flake atoms that passivates some unpaired electrons. Magnetic properties depend also on stacking, for instance pristine bilayer triangular flakes become antiferromagnetic due to pairing between edge states. The energy gap significantly affected by doping, for instance the gap decreases from ~3.7 eV in hexagonal flakes to 1.5 eV when it is doped with Na in the upper position. Electrons from the broken bonds around the doped metal form orbitals loosely bound to the flake consequently decreasing the energy gap. On the other hand, stacking increases the energy gap in bilayer triangular flakes due to the mutual passivation of the reactive edge states from both the layers. The calculated spin up/down density ratio is high in hexagonal flakes leading to high spin polarization (P), for instance P = 0.64 in hexagonal flakes doped with K atom. The found enhanced spin polarization, in addition to the tunable magnetic properties, makes doped graphene flakes promising candidates for spintronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
DFT
Doping
Alkali metals
Electronic and magnetic properties
Spin polarization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The positions at which the alkali metals are attached or embedded within the AHEX (a) and ZTRI (b) quantum dots.
Optimized structures for single and bilayer AHEX and ZTRI graphene quantum dots chemically modified with Li, Na, and K.
The electronic density of states of single layer AHEX flake before and after doping (a–g) and bilayer BAHEX flake (h, i).

The alpha HOMO/LUMO density distribution for doped AHEX (a–f) and for BAHEX (g, h). The positive and negative signs of the molecular orbital wave function are denoted by (online only) the dark purple and the green color, respectively. The molecular orbital isovalue is 0.002.
Spin density distribution of doped AHEX and ZTRI flakes. Blue/green color represents alpha/beta spin density, the isodensity value is 0.0004.

The calculated C–C bond length (dCC), Metal–C bond length (dCX,), binding energy (EB), Hirshfeld charges on dopants (ΔQ), HOMO–LUMO energy gap (Eg), and total electric dipole moment (TEDM) for doped single and bilayer AHEX.
The same as in Table 1 but for ZTRI.
The spin polarization (P) and difference between ground state energy of relevant spin states (ΔE) of doped GQDs.

head.ce:title: Electronic and magnetic properties of graphene quantum dots doped with alkali metals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The electronic and magnetic properties of armchair-hexagonal (AHEX) and zigzag-triangular (ZTRI) graphene quantum dots doped with alkali metals are investigated using density functional theory. The binding energy confirms the stability of the undoped systems. Although doping decreases stability of single layer structures, in bilayer ones the binding energy between the layers increases. The former is due to the broken bonds and the deformation at the surface, while the later is due to the chemical bonds formation between the layers. We found that the lowest ground state energy for AHEX/AHEX-doped is the singlet/quadruplet spin state. Therefore, AHEX dots experience transformation from diamagnetic to ferromagnetic state after doping. In addition, the optimized spin state for ZTRI/ZTRI-doped is the quintet/sextet spin-polarized state. In few cases, the doped AHEX/ZTRI dots have doublet/quadruplet spin state due to the strong interaction between the flake atoms that passivates some unpaired electrons. Magnetic properties depend also on stacking, for instance pristine bilayer triangular flakes become antiferromagnetic due to pairing between edge states. The energy gap significantly affected by doping, for instance the gap decreases from ~3.7 eV in hexagonal flakes to 1.5 eV when it is doped with Na in the upper position. Electrons from the broken bonds around the doped metal form orbitals loosely bound to the flake consequently decreasing the energy gap. On the other hand, stacking increases the energy gap in bilayer triangular flakes due to the mutual passivation of the reactive edge states from both the layers. The calculated spin up/down density ratio is high in hexagonal flakes leading to high spin polarization (P), for instance P = 0.64 in hexagonal flakes doped with K atom. The found enhanced spin polarization, in addition to the tunable magnetic properties, makes doped graphene flakes promising candidates for spintronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Since 2004 to date “graphene”, single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal structure, has shown many unique characteristics such as high mechanical flexibility, high conductivity, low resistivity, and excellent thermal and chemical stability [1–3]. These unique characteristics make graphene attractive to a variety of applications, including electronics [1,4], energy related systems [5,6], sensors [7,8], actuators [9,10], and composites [11,12]. However, the zero-energy gap of graphene hinders its application in different fields, for instance the possibility to observe its optical photoluminescence (PL) is highly improbable, thus limiting its application in optoelectronics and photonics. One approach to open the energy gap is to reduce the lateral size of graphene to produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [13–15]. Once the energy gap is open, GQDs become interesting candidates for electronic devices. Due to their intensive quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs exhibit extraordinary properties such as low toxicity, high biocompatibility, good photostability and solubility [4,5].

The electronic properties and catalytic activity of graphene can be effectively controlled by doping with heteroatoms [16]. Similar to opening energy gap in graphene by reducing the lateral size, doping with heteroatoms can also open energy gap which in turn transforms graphene from metallic to semiconductor material [17]. A wide range of atoms, e.g. nitrogen, phosphorus, and germanium, has been employed for the doping process which widens the spectrum of doped graphene in different applications [18–21]. Interactions of dopant atoms with C-atoms strongly depend on the atomic nature of the dopant. For instance, nitrogen that has atomic radius and bond length similar to carbon will substitute C atom with negligible deformation in graphene lattice while for atoms with large atomic radii, such as germanium, it forms sp3-like bonding with C-atoms with noticeable deformation. With respect to GQDs, it has been recently shown that their electronic and chemical properties can be modified by doping and chemical functionalization that leads to outstanding properties that can be utilized in electronic and spintronic [22–25], optoelectronic [26–28], and environmental applications [29,30].

In this work, a comparative DFT study is performed to investigate the possible enhancement and tunability that alkali metals, namely Li, Na, and K, can introduce to the electronic and magnetic properties of single and bilayer GQDs. The reason of choosing alkali metals here is the reactive unpaired electrons that they can provide. These electrons break the balance between the spin up and spin down electrons, which in addition to the substitutional doping strongly boosts the spintronic properties of graphene flakes. The electronic properties can be also tuned toward the desired application by alkali metals. In addition to the substitutional doping, alkali metals are attached to the edges of the flakes to compare their effect on GQDs in both cases. The effect of position is also studied by placing the metal at different positions inside the flake. The next section presents the theoretical model, section 3 the results and discussions, and in section 4 the conclusions are given.

The hexagonal GQDs with armchair termination (AHEX) and the triangular with zigzag edge (ZTRI) graphene are chosen as the model flakes in this study as indicated in Fig. 1 (a, b). The total number of atoms (carbon plus hydrogen) in AHEX is 60 atoms (42-carbon and 18 hydrogen) and 64 in ZTRI (46-carbon and18-hydrogen). As shown in the figure the alkali metals (Li, Na, K) are attached to the position E of the flake or substitute a carbon atom within the flake at the upper (U) or the lower (L) hexagon vertex position. Only one alkali atom is used in the edge functionalization or substitutional doping, therefore the total number of atoms in AHEX and ZTRI before and after modification is the same. The acronym AHEX-X represents X alkali metal (X = Li, Na, or K) attached to the E position while the AHEX-P- X indicates doped AHEX with X alkali atom in the P position, with P= U or L.

The density functional theory (DFT) calculations are executed in Gaussian 16 [31] by using the Becke-three Parameters-Lee-Yang-Parr (B3LYP) hybrid functional [32,33] that showed a good representation of the electronic structure of C-based nanosystems [17,18,34]. The basis set 3–21g∗ is used in the calculations [35,36], here we compare between the effect of the present basis (3–21g∗) and a higher basis set (6–311g∗) to confirm the adequacy of the 3–21g∗ basis set. Our example will be the AHEX-U-Na, shown in Fig. 2 (g). The energy gap and the time taken for the calculations using B3LYP/3-21g∗ are Eg = 2.255 eV and T = 100 min, respectively. While when using B3LYP/6-311g∗, Eg = 2.257 eV and T = 559 min. Thus, 3–21g∗ is an adequate basis set when considering both the calculations accuracy and the computational power. The used DFT level of theory is also experimentally justified in previous works on graphene-based materials. The HOMO–LUMO energy gap of carboxylate graphene flakes calculated at this level of theory equals 3.76 [24] which is comparable to the experimentally measured optical gap~3 eV [37]. Moreover, for chevron graphene nanoribbons the current calculation level shows good agreement with experimental results [38].

The optimized structures of AHEX and ZTRI nanoflakes doped with alkali metals (Li, Na, and K) are shown in Fig. 2. Fig. 2 (a-c) shows the optimized structure of AHEX-Li, AHEX-Na, and AHEX-K, respectively, where the alkali atoms are attached to the E position.

The optimized structures of the doped AHEX in the L and U position are shown in Fig. 2 (d-i). The relaxed structures of AHEX bilayer (BAHEX) with AB stacking is aslo shown before (Fig. 2 j) and after chemical modification (Fig. 2 k-m). The second part of Fig. 2 presents the relaxed ZTRI structures after the attachment/doping of the alkali metals following the same strategy as used for AHEX. It is important to indicate that the Van der Waals force between layers in bilayer systems and in single layer between alkali metals and GQDs are considered in our calculation by adding Grimmes's dispersion correction (gd3) to the b3lyb functional [39].

In this subsection, we study the effect of doping with alkali metals and layer-stacking on the shape and the stability of the resultant structures. The stability of the resultant structures is tested by calculating the binding energy (EB) per atom. The binding energy is given by: EB = [NH∗EH+(NC-NX)∗EC + NX∗Ex- ES]/Ns, where NH, NC, NX, and NS is the number of H, C, doped metal, and all atoms, respectively. EH, EC, EX, and ES are the corresponding ground state energies, respectively. In case of edge passivation, EB is the same as previous but with NX subtracted from NH because in this case NX replaces H atom not C atom. In the case of bilayer, the two layers binding energy (which is different from the atomic EB in single layer) is the ground state energy of the single layers minus the ground state energy of the bilayer. According to this definition, a positive binding energy implies that the resultant structures are stable; therefore, from the positive values (see Tables 1 and 2
) of EB we conclude that all the structures under investigations are stable.

It is clearly seen from Fig. 2 (a-c) that the effect of attaching alkali metals to the edge of the GQDs has a slight effect on the geometrical shape. The C–C bond lengths (dcc) given in Table 1 for AHEX attached to Li, Na, and K ranges from 1.39 to 1.47 Å which is similar to that of the unmodified AHEX, similarly for dcc in Table 2 for ZTRI [36]. Also, their binding energies (EB) have almost the same strength like that of AHEX or ZTRI. In deep contrast, the effect of alkali metals on the structure strongly increases when they replace one of the flake's carbon atoms, i.e. act as dopants. This effect is observed in the considerable changes in the dCC and dCX from the original dCC in AHEX or ZTRI, for example in AHEX-L-K dCC ranges from 1.35 to 1.53 in addition to dCX ~1.77–2.89 Å which replace some of C–C bonds imply that a considerable structural change occurs in the AHEX. The highest deformation observed in AHEX doped with K atom in the upper or the lower position as shown in Fig. 2 (h, i). The deformation is a result of two main reasons (a) alkali atoms provide only one electron for bonding with surface C-atoms (b) the high atomic radii of the alkali atoms with respect to C-atoms, both reasons make the GQDs to look like flakes with a surface vacancy having the dopant inside. Consequently, the binding energy decreases in the doping process, EB decreases from 7.1 eV in AHEX to 6.7 eV in AHEX-U-K. Therefore, the doping increases the deformation and decreases the binding strength of the structures.

The binding strength dramatically decreases in bilayer AHEX (BAHEX) with EB~ 0.05 eV because EB in this case represents binding between layers not between atoms. The attachment of the dopant to the U position, as shown in Fig. 2 (m) for BAHEX-U-Na, increases the binding energy, namely from EB = 0.05 in BAHEX to EB = 0.22 eV in the doped BAHEX-U-Na. The Na atoms move to the region between the two layers decreasing the layer separation and forming additional bonds which in turn increases EB. Therefore, doping single layer AHEX with alkali metals decreases the binding energy while it provides a noticeable enhancement to the binding energy in bilayer BAHEX. The binding energy of BZTRI is comparable to BAHEX because of the comparable distance between the layers ~3.3 Å. The binding energy of BZTRI also increases by doping with the highest EB = 0.26 eV in BZTRI-U-K as in Table 2.

The electronic and magnetic properties will be considered through investigations of the effects of doping and stacking on the density of states (DOS), highest occupied/lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO/LUMO), energy gap, spin states, and spin polarization of AHEX and ZTRI quantum dots. DOS is obtained by using Gaussian function for each energy level,
f
( ε )
=
1
2 π
σ 2
e
−
( ε −
ε i
)
2
2
σ 2
, with broadening
σ = 0.03
e V
. Fig. 3 shows the DOS of AHEX (Fig. 3 a, only for comparison) and selected doped and layered structures. The position of the metal significantly affects the HOMO–LUMO energy gap (Eg) and the total electric dipole moment (TEDM) of the GQDs. Let us first discuss the effect of attaching alkali metals to the edges of the AHEX. As given in Table 1 the effect is a moderate decrease of Eg and a considerable enhancement of the TEDM. The highly reactive alkali metal at the edge gives its single electron to the AHEX carbon atom to form a bond with length that is relatively larger than the C–C bond length which means that its energy is smaller, thus the energy gap decreases. The largest Metal–C bond found was the K–C bond in the AHEX-K, consequently the narrowest gap, Eg = 2.6 eV. This conclusion is confirmed by the HOMO/LUMO distribution around the K atom shown in Fig. 4 a, b. The large difference in electronegativity between C atom (2.55) and alkali metals (Li = 0.98, Na = 0.93, K = 0.82) leads to high charge transfer from the metal to the flake which in turn increases the TEDM. The positive values of ΔQ in Table 1 mean that charges are transferred from the alkali metal to graphene flake. The highest charge transfer occurs from K-atom in AHEX-K consequently this structure has the highest TEDM~12.54 (D). When the alkali metals are embedded into the GQDs, the net charge on the alkali metal decreases where in this case the charge transfer occurs not only with one C-atoms as in the case of attachment at the edges but rather with several C-atoms at the surface thus the total charge on the dopant decreases. These changes in the charge transfer as a result of changing the dopant type and position have been observed also in doped pyrene-based devices and can be used to control the transport properties [40,41].

The HOMO–LUMO energy gap (Eg) experiences considerable decrease depending on the type and the position of the metal as indicated in Table 1. The AHEX undergoes transformation from a wide energy gap semiconductor (Eg ~3.7 eV in AHEX) to a narrow gap semiconductor (Eg ~2.1 eV in AHEX-L-Li) and so on reaching Eg ~1.5 eV in AHEX-U-K, as depicted in Fig. 3. As previously mentioned for edge modified GQDs, the energy gap decreases due to the small bond energy between Metal-C compared to the C–C one. In the case of doping the energy gap experiences stronger decrease due to the fact that the broken bonds around the metal leave electrons loosely bound to the GQDs.

The position of the dopant also affects the HOMO–LUMO energy gap, for example AHEX-L-Li has Eg = 2.1 eV while AHEX-U-Li has Eg = 1.2 eV. In the first case, where Li is closer to the edge atoms, the doping slightly changes the C–C bonds while in the second case, Li atom is closer to the center of the flake, the C–C bond length increases reaching 1.55 Å (see Table 1) so the corresponding bond energy decreases. Thus the effect of doping in the upper position decreases the energy gap more than the L position. This conclusion is valid for Li and Na but for K the dCC is comparable in both cases so the energy gap is comparable too. The noticeable increase in some of the C–C bond energy after doping can be seen by comparing the HOMO/LUMO in Fig. 4a (edge modified AHEX) with that in Fig. 4 b-e (doped AHEX). In the second case the distribution is on the metals and the C atoms with respect to the distribution only around metal in the first case. On the other hand, the TEDM greatly decreases, for instance, in AHEX-Na the TEDM~ 10 D while in AHEX-U-Na it equals 5 D. This diminution is a result of the cancelation of local dipoles in opposite directions around the dopant leading to a decrease in the TEDM.

The energy gap of BAHEX is similar to that of AHEX implying a slight effect of stacking on the band structures. The attached K atoms to BAHEX do not passivate the C-edge atoms as in single layer, however the interaction between the two layers moves them far from their passions at the edges (see Fig. 2 e) which in turn leads to a higher decrease in the energy gap with respect to single layer attached to K atoms. In case of BAHEX-U-Na, the new C–C bonds between the two layers have lower energy comparing to the C–C bonds inside the layer so the resultant states will form the low energy states and decrease the energy gap. This conclusion is confirmed by the HOMO distribution around the bonds between the two layers shown in Fig. 4 h.

An important influence of doping on AHEX is the transformation from nonmagnetic to ferromagnetic (FM) state after doping as seen in Fig. 3 b-f. This transformation originates from (a) the nature of the alkali metals where single electron from Li, Na, or K breaks the symmetry between the spin up and spin down states and (b) the removal of one C-atom generates two unpaired electrons in adjacent atoms. Thus, the expected total spin of doped AHEX is 1.5, one unpaired electron from (a) and two from (b) see the encircled charges in Fig. 2 (e). However, in some cases the total spin in doped AHEX decreased to 0.5 due to the interaction between inner C-atoms that passivate their unpaired electrons as seen in Fig. 2 (g). The nonmagnetic state of AHEX is confirmed by performing the following three spin states: spin-unpolarized singlet state, spin-polarized single state, and spin polarized triplet state. We found that the lowest ground state among these three states is the spin-unpolarized singlet states. In addition, the comparison between doublet, quartet, and sextet spin states in doped AHEX is performed to identify the spin state of all the flakes and will be discussed in details in Table 3 .

Now we turn on to study the effect of doping and layer stacking on the electronic and magnetic properties of ZTRI. Similar to AHEX attaching alkali metals attached to the edges increases the charge transfer and total dipole moment of the flakes. In contrast to AHEX and due to the geometrical shape the alkali metals in doped ZTRI are out of the plane interacting with lower number of surface atoms with in turn enhancement and charge transfer and TEDM as given in Table 2. The electronic density of states of single and bilayer ZTRI are shown in Figure 6, Appendix A. Similar to AHEX, the HOMO–LUMO energy gap of ZTRI experiences a slight decrease by attaching alkali atoms to the edges and considerable decrease with alkali atom doping, as given in Table 2. The HOMO/LUMO distribution in BZTRI (Figure 7 Appendix A) is over all the structure which means that the reactive edge states disappear in BZTRI due to the pairing between these energy states from both layers leading to increase of energy gap, namely Eg = 1.66 eV. This pairing leads also to transformation from FM state to AFM state, see Fig. 5 K.

The characteristic edge states of ZTRI, which depend on the size of the quantum dot, make it a ferromagnetic material. The current calculations show that the lowest ground state energy of ZTRI is the quintet spin-polarized state with total spin of 2. Attaching alkali atoms to the edges does not change the spin state of the flakes while substitutional doping increases the spin to 2.5 or decreases it to 1.5. The spin state of ZTRI after removing one C-atom from the L or the U positions is the quintet state. Therefore, by doping with alkali atoms the expected spin state is the sextet state. However, in some cases the spin state decreases to quartet state due to the induced interactions by doping similar to AHEX. In Table 3, the energy difference (ΔE) between quartet and doublet spin states for AHEX and the difference between sextet and quartet states for ZTRI is calculated to identify the spin state and its relative stability. A negative value of ΔE represents quartet/sextet spin state in doped AHEX/ZTRI while a positive value represents doublet/quartet. ΔE indicates also the stability of the magnetic states, for instance the stability of the quartet state in AHEX-L-Li is higher than in AHEX-U-Li with ΔE = −0.39 eV in the former and −0.033 eV in the latter. In doped BZTRI, the triplet state has a comparable stability with respect to the singlet state. In Appendix B, we show that the electronic and magnetic properties obtained in this work can be applied to quantum dots with different sizes through investigating AHEX and ZTRI with larger sizes.

To provide additional study on the effect of doping on the magnetic properties, we plot the spin density distribution for doped AHEX and ZTRI in Fig. 5. It is observed that the spin densities are localized around the dopant metals and the nearby C atoms in AHEX, which indicates that the origin of the ferromagnetism in these flakes are the active unpaired electrons from the alkali metals and the neighboring C-atoms. The spin up density is dominant, as shown by the blue cubs in Fig. 5 a-f, in all the doped flakes except for the AHEX-U-Li/Na (Fig. 5 d, e) where both the spin-up and spin-down densities are comparable. These comparable spin up/down densities will decrease the spin polarization in this flakes with respect to other AHEX flakes. In case of ZTRI, the spin densities distribute over the entire flake as seen in Fig. 5 g. The edge states electrons of ZTRI are highly reactive like that from the alkali metals and therefore the spin density distributes over the edge and surface of the flake (see Fig. 5 g-j). The spin-up and spin-down have almost the same densities in all doped ZTRI flakes, which in turn result in law spin polarization. It is worth noting that in bilayer flakes, the magnetic properties changes from single layers, for instance the nonmagnetic AHEX becomes AFM in BAHEX-U-Na (Fig. 5 f) and the ferromagnetic ZTRI becomes AFM in BZTR with spin up on one layer and spin down densities on the other layer (Fig. 5 k). The spin polarization (P) is calculated from the following equation:
P =
N u
−
N d
N u
+
N d
where
N u
a n d
N d
are the Mullikan densities of the spin-up and spin-down, respectively. The calculated values of P in Table 3 show a huge diversity depending on the shape/edge termination (AHEX/ZTRI), doped metal, and its position.

The ZTRI flakes have lower spin polarization compared to AHEX as result of the equal distribution of spin up/down densities. With respect to the metal and its position it is found that K has the largest value of spin polarization, namely P = 0.64 in the L-position as given in Table 3 for AHEX-L-K.

The magnetic and electronic properties of doped armchair hexagonal (AHEX) and zigzag triangular (ZTRI) single and bilayer graphene quantum dots have been investigated using density functional theory. The dopants are the alkali metals (Li, Na, and K) that substitute one of the C-atoms inside the flake in different positions. Also they are attached to the edges to compare their effects on the GQDs in both cases. The calculated positive binding energy implies that all the resultant structures are stable. The geometrical shape, electronic, and magnetic properties of AHEX and ZTRI are slightly affected by attaching alkali metals to their edges while in case of doping the structures experience noticeable alternations. With respect to shape, the flake deformation due to doping is higher than the case of edge functionalization leading to a decrease in the binding energy. The bilayer systems (BAHEX and BZTRI) have comparable binding energy between the layers that can be enhanced by doping due to the strong bonds formation between atoms from both layers. The density of states show that the HOMO–LUMO energy gap substantially affected by the type and position of the doped metal. For instance, the energy gap in AHEX decreases from 3.7 eV to 2.1 eV when Li attached to the left position and to 1.2 eV when Li is attached to the upper position. The broken bonds around the doped metal form molecular orbitals with electrons loosely bound to the flake, thus decrease the energy gap. This conclusion is supported by the HOMO/LUMO distribution around the alkali atoms in all the cases. On the other hand, the HOMO–LUMO energy gap increases in pristine BZTRI due to the passivation of the interactive edge states by each other from both the layers.

The magnetic properties show a significant diversity depending on the shape, dopant, and the stacking. The spin calculations show that the lowest ground state energy for AHEX is the singlet spin-unpolarized state. Attaching alkali metals to the edges does not change the magnetic state of the flake, however substitutional doping increases the magnetic state to doublet or quartet depending on the interactions between C-atoms around the dopant. Therefore, substitutional doping converts the nonmagnetic AHEX to ferromagnetic with total spin of 1.5 originated from the unpaired electrons of the alkali metal and neighbor C-atoms or 0.5 from only the dopant. The optimized spin state of ZTRI is the quintet state due to the presence of edge states in triangular flakes with zigzag edges that spin state can be increased or decreased by doping depending also on the strength of the interactions among the flake atoms. Thus, ZTRI with 46 C-atoms have a ferromagnetic state with total spin of two that increases to 2.5 or, in few cases, decreases to 1.5 by doping with one alkali atom. Magnetic properties depend also on stacking where pristine bilayer triangular flakes become antiferromagnetic due to pairing between edge states. Investigations of spin density distribution and spin polarization show that spin up/down densities are distributed around the doped metals with high ratio of spin up in AHEX and comparable ratio in ZTRI. The spin polarization (P) is then higher in AHEX due to the higher spin up density. The highest value of spin polarization achieved is P = 0.64 in AHEX doped with K atom in the L-position. Thus, the significant enhancement of the spin polarization and the tunable magnetic properties make doped GQDs interesting for spintronic devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research utilized IAU's Bridge HPC facility, supported by IAU Scientific and High Performance Computing Center. Also supported by NNSF of China (No. 11774178), NSFJP (20KJA430004), and RCAMS-KKU (RCAMS/KKU/017–20).

ce:section.ce:label: APPENDIX A
ce:section.ce:para:
The electronic properties of ZTRI strongly depend on the type and position of the alkali metal, and layer number as observed in Fig. A1.
Fig. A1
Density of states of ZTRI before and after doping (a-f) and BZTRI (g, i). The arrows indicate the HOMO and LUMO.
Fig. A1

The effect of doping ZTRI with alkali metals on the distribution of HOMO/LUMO is presented in Fig. A2. The edge distribution of HOMO/LUMO characterizing the ZTRI significantly changed by the doping or by adding a second layer to form BZTRI.
Fig. A2
HOMO/LUMO distribution of doped ZTRI (a-f) and BZTRI (g, h).
Fig. A2

ce:section.ce:label: APPENDIX B
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to generalize the conclusions in the current work to other systems with different sizes, we investigate the electronic and magnetic properties of larger structures. The large AHEX and ZTRI consist of a total number of atoms equals 144 and 102 atoms, respectively. The electronic and magnetic properties of these structures after the substitutional doping with one Li atom in the u positions are investigated and shown in Fig. A3. The results show that AHEX has a diamagnetic ordering (spin singlet state) that becomes ferromagnetic ordered by Li doping with spin distributed around the dopant (Fig. A3) similar to the results for smaller AHEX. In addition, the energy gap, Eg = 2.39 eV, decreases by doping to Eg = 1.49 eV. This decrease is a result of the formation of low energy orbitals (HOMO in Fig. A3) within the gap by the C-atoms around the doping position. The ZTRI case shows that it has intrinsic ferromagnetic ordering with septet spin state (S = 3) and the spin density distribution is higher on the edge atoms as seen in Fig. A3. This spin value is higher than that of the smaller ZTRI (S = 2) because the larger ZTRI has seven edge hexagons that increases the spin to 3 (S=(n-1)/2 with n = 7 is the number of edge hexagons). Doping with Li in the U position increases the spin state to the octet state due to the addition of the unpaired electron from Li, as can be seen from Fig. A3 where the additional spin down cubes (blue cubes) distribute on Li and around it. Therefore, the physicals properties obtained in this work can be applied to other graphene quantum dots with different sizes.
Fig. A3
Optimized structures, HOMO, and spin density distributions of Li doped GQDs with sizes different from that considered in the text.
Fig. A3
